response_raw:
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"When I see things online, especially about serious topics or even just recommendations for a new place to visit, my first instinct is always to pause. It makes me feel anxious sometimes, because you never know what’s real and what someone has made up. Since I don't spend too much time on these platforms—maybe only a few times a month when looking at photos from friends or planning a little trip—I try to remember that social media is just one small window into the world, and it can be very misleading. It’s like getting a postcard; you get a lovely picture, but you don't get to smell the sea air or feel the cobblestones under your feet in Bari. 

The best way I’ve found to manage all this fake content is really about slowing down my own reaction and being gentle with myself while doing it. Instead of getting upset by sensational headlines—which are usually designed just to make you click—I try a few simple checks. First, I always check who posted it. Do they have a history of sharing reliable things? Are they friends or people I trust? If the source is unknown, I treat it like something I haven't seen before and pass over it. 

Secondly, if a piece of information seems too perfect, or too outrageous to be true, then chances are it isn't. For example, if someone posts a miracle cure for everything that sounds almost unbelievable, I know I need more proof than just one picture. Instead of taking what I see at face value—especially when my heart is beating fast because I feel worried about something—I try to look for multiple sources. I might do a quick search on Google or ask a friend who knows a bit about the subject. It’s not about being suspicious of everyone, but rather protecting myself from feeling overwhelmed by panic or misinformation that isn't based in truth. 

Finally, and this is important, I remember my own core beliefs and what brings me peace—like spending time photographing beautiful places or knitting a warm blanket. Those real activities are always more grounding than scrolling through endless feeds of questionable claims. By focusing on the tangible things in life—the taste of good coffee, the warmth of family gathering, or the beauty captured during a walk—I can keep my mind from being hijacked by what is fake online. It takes effort, but it keeps me feeling calm and grounded.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
When I see things online, especially about serious topics or even just recommendations for a new place to visit, my first instinct is always to pause. It makes me feel anxious sometimes, because you never know what’s real and what someone has made up. Since I don't spend too much time on these platforms—maybe only a few times a month when looking at photos from friends or planning a little trip—I try to remember that social media is just one small window into the world, and it can be very misleading. It’s like getting a postcard; you get a lovely picture, but you don't get to smell the sea air or feel the cobblestones under your feet in Bari. 

The best way I’ve found to manage all this fake content is really about slowing down my own reaction and being gentle with myself while doing it. Instead of getting upset by sensational headlines—which are usually designed just to make you click—I try a few simple checks. First, I always check who posted it. Do they have a history of sharing reliable things? Are they friends or people I trust? If the source is unknown, I treat it like something I haven't seen before and pass over it. 

Secondly, if a piece of information seems too perfect, or too outrageous to be true, then chances are it isn't. For example, if someone posts a miracle cure for everything that sounds almost unbelievable, I know I need more proof than just one picture. Instead of taking what I see at face value—especially when my heart is beating fast because I feel worried about something—I try to look for multiple sources. I might do a quick search on Google or ask a friend who knows a bit about the subject. It’s not about being suspicious of everyone, but rather protecting myself from feeling overwhelmed by panic or misinformation that isn't based in truth. 

Finally, and this is important, I remember my own core beliefs and what brings me peace—like spending time photographing beautiful places or knitting a warm blanket. Those real activities are always more grounding than scrolling through endless feeds of questionable claims. By focusing on the tangible things in life—the taste of good coffee, the warmth of family gathering, or the beauty captured during a walk—I can keep my mind from being hijacked by what is fake online. It takes effort, but it keeps me feeling calm and grounded.
